questions.question: What are the three mechanical operations that dominate HDD latency?
questions.correctAnswer: Seek (move head to track), rotational latency (wait for sector to spin under head), and transfer (read data)
questions.explanation: Seek ~8-9ms, rotational ~4ms (7200 RPM), transfer ~16µs for 4KB. 99.9% of latency is mechanical.

questions.question: Why is sequential access 100-750x faster than random access on HDD?
options: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.correctAnswer: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.explanation: Sequential: 250 MB/s. Random 4KB: ~0.33 MB/s (~83 IOPS × 4KB). Ratio ~750:1. Entirely due to mechanical seek+rotation.

questions.question: What does 'Advanced Format' / 4K native sectors mean?
options: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.correctAnswer: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.explanation: 512e drives: logical 512, physical 4096. A 512-byte write triggers RMW of full 4KB sector. Align partitions to 4KB.

questions.question: What is the areal density of modern HDDs?
options: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.correctAnswer: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.explanation: Higher areal density = more bits pass under the head per rotation. Primary driver of HDD throughput improvements.

questions.question: Why is append-only writing fast on HDD?
options: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.correctAnswer: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.explanation: Append = sequential write at current position. In-place = seek + rotational latency. Difference: 100-1000x.

questions.question: What is the fundamental asymmetry of NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: Read/program per page (~4-16KB), erase per block (hundreds of pages). Cannot overwrite — must erase first.
acceptableAnswers: read program per page erase per block
read and write per page, erase per block
questions.explanation: Page = read/write unit (~25-100µs read, ~200-1500µs write). Block = erase unit (512-2048 pages, ~1.5-10ms).

questions.question: What is write amplification?
options: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.correctAnswer: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.explanation: Overwrite → old page stale, write to new page. GC: copy valid pages, erase block. User 4KB may cause 2MB+ of NAND writes.

questions.question: What does the Flash Translation Layer (FTL) do?
options: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.correctAnswer: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.explanation: FTL = SSD's firmware brain. LBA→physical mapping, wear leveling, GC, bad block management. OS sees a block device.

questions.question: Why does SLC endure 50-100x more writes than QLC?
options: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.correctAnswer: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.explanation: SLC: 50K-100K P/E cycles. QLC: ~500-1000. More bits per cell = tighter voltage discrimination = faster wear-out.

questions.question: Why do SSDs slow down when nearly full?
options: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.correctAnswer: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.explanation: 90% full SSD: few pre-erased blocks. GC must reclaim before writes. Over-provisioning (7-28% hidden spare) mitigates.

questions.question: Why is NVMe faster than SATA for SSDs?
options: NVMe: 65,536 queues × 65,536 commands, direct PCIe connection. SATA: 1 queue × 32 commands, AHCI overhead
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe: 65,536 queues × 65,536 commands, direct PCIe connection. SATA: 1 queue × 32 commands, AHCI overhead
questions.explanation: SATA designed for single-actuator HDDs (one thing at a time). NVMe designed for SSD parallelism (many NAND dies in parallel).

questions.question: What is the practical bandwidth of PCIe 4.0 ×4?
questions.correctAnswer: ~7-8 GB/s (16 GT/s × 4 lanes × 128b/130b encoding)
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s per lane, 128b/130b → ~1.97 GB/s per lane → ~7.88 GB/s for ×4. Real NVMe ~7 GB/s after overhead.

questions.question: What does the M.2 key tell you?
options: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes). B-key = SATA. B+M = either. The notch position encodes the protocol.
questions.correctAnswer: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes). B-key = SATA. B+M = either. The notch position encodes the protocol.
questions.explanation: M.2 is just the connector form factor. Always check: NVMe M.2 (PCIe) vs SATA M.2 (600 MB/s bottleneck).

questions.question: How many PCIe lanes does a typical consumer CPU have?
options: ~20-28 (GPU takes 16, leaving 4-12 for NVMe SSDs and peripherals)
questions.correctAnswer: ~20-28 (GPU takes 16, leaving 4-12 for NVMe SSDs and peripherals)
questions.explanation: Lane budget is fixed in silicon. Populating multiple M.2 slots may disable SATA ports or bifurcate GPU ×16 to ×8.

questions.question: What is the NVMe command set advantage over AHCI?
options: ~50% fewer CPU cycles to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
questions.correctAnswer: ~50% fewer CPU cycles to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
questions.explanation: NVMe command set is streamlined. AHCI carries ATA legacy overhead. NVMe ≈ half the CPU cost per I/O submission.

questions.question: What is an inode in Unix filesystems?
options: A fixed-size data structure identified by number — stores file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size data structure identified by number — stores file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
questions.explanation: Every file/directory has one inode. Filename lives in directory entry, not the inode. Hard links = multiple directory entries → same inode.

questions.question: What does the page cache do?
options: Caches disk data in RAM — first read hits disk, subsequent reads hit cache (~100ns vs ~10ms HDD)
questions.correctAnswer: Caches disk data in RAM — first read hits disk, subsequent reads hit cache (~100ns vs ~10ms HDD)
questions.explanation: Page cache = kernel-managed DRAM caching disk blocks. Read: check cache first. Write: write to cache, mark dirty, flush async.

questions.question: What does fsync() guarantee that write() alone does not?
options: write() goes to page cache only (volatile). fsync() forces dirty pages to durable storage before returning.
questions.correctAnswer: write() goes to page cache only (volatile). fsync() forces dirty pages to durable storage before returning.
questions.explanation: write() is fast (microseconds) but data is only in RAM. Power loss = data loss. fsync() blocks until disk confirms write.

questions.question: How does journaling prevent filesystem corruption after a crash?
options: Write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying structures. On reboot, committed entries are replayed.
questions.correctAnswer: Write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying structures. On reboot, committed entries are replayed.
questions.explanation: Journal: write intent → commit → do work → mark done. Crash: replay committed entries. Inconsistent states become consistent.

questions.question: Why is 'free RAM' misleading on Linux?
options: Much of 'used' RAM is page cache — instantly evictable if an application needs it. Unused RAM IS wasted RAM.
questions.correctAnswer: Much of 'used' RAM is page cache — instantly evictable if an application needs it. Unused RAM IS wasted RAM.
questions.explanation: Page cache = 'used' but available. Kernel will evict clean pages instantly. High free RAM = you have more RAM than your working set.
